docs\md11_phase2_v2_threat_model.md:86:`ModelPin.env_snapshot` uses `None` to mean "var was unset
docs\md11_phase2_v2_threat_model.md:87:at capture ti…252144 tokens truncated…surface systems [CITE:ev_f61ee522cf2a7217] (see also: Soil-Porewater Partitioning Mechanisms in Unsaturated Zones). Research on unsaturated soils demonstrates the importance of air-water interfacial adsorption for distribution of longer-chain PFAS within soil matrices, with implications for porewater mobility and potential groundwater migration [CITE:ev_7a39778821ebcf06] (see also: Air-Water Interfacial Dynamics: Differential Accumulation by Chain Length).\n\nThese observations illuminate fundamental constraints on foam-based remediation strategies. Technologies optimized for long-chain PFAS removal underperform when applied to short-chain-dominated contamination scenarios [CITE:ev_f61ee522cf2a7217]. The solution retention exhibited by short-chain compounds ensures their continued presence in groundwater, facilitating transport through porous media and expanding contaminant plumes [CITE:ev_f61ee522cf2a7217]. Treatment system design must account for this chain-length-dependent fractionation susceptibility, potentially necessitating sequential or hybrid approaches addressing both compound classes [CITE:ev_f61ee522cf2a7217].\n\nThe connection between surface activity and leaching potential operates through multiple pathways. Reduced foam fractionation efficiency for short-chain PFAS implies these compounds persist longer in aqueous phases, sustaining driving gradients for vertical migration through unsaturated zones [CITE:ev_f61ee522cf2a7217] (see also: Nationwide Environmental Distribution Patterns Across Chain Lengths). Meanwhile, long-chain PFAS removed through interfacial processes undergo reconcentration in foamate, requiring subsequent treatment or disposal that introduces secondary management challenges [CITE:ev_f61ee522cf2a7217]. The differential solution retention characteristics thus shape not only immediate treatment outcomes but also long-term contaminant fate and transport trajectories within aquifer systems [CITE:ev_f61ee522cf2a7217].\n\n## Soil-Porewater Partitioning Mechanisms in Unsaturated Zones\n\n The vadose zone functions as the critical interface determining PFAS fate prior to groundwater contamination, with chain-length-dependent partitioning governing whether compounds remain sequestered in soil or migrate downward [CITE:ev_f465951926ec5f89]. This decoupling between total soil burden and leachable fraction creates fundamental uncertainty in risk assessment methodologies that rely on bulk soil measurements.\n\nWhile interfacial sequestration at air-water boundaries temporarily retards vertical migration of long-chain compounds, this same mechanism creates a reservoir subject to mobilization upon water table fluctuations or infiltration events [CITE:ev_7a39778821ebcf06]—a dynamic discussed in relation to secondary source formation in Sediment Contamination Hotspots: Formation and Secondary Release Dynamics. Short-chain compounds, lacking comparable interfacial affinity, maintain higher effective mobility through the vadose profile despite lower individual sorption affinities per unit surface area.\n\nSorption-desorption kinetics display pronounced hysteresis, with desorption rates systematically slower than sorption rates for both chain-length classes [CITE:ev_f465951926ec5f89]. The magnitude of this kinetic asymmetry increases with perfluorocarbon chain length, reflecting stronger van der Waals interactions between fluorinated tails and soil surfaces [CITE:ev_f465951926ec5f89]—consistent with the chain-length-dependent stability patterns examined in Chemical Persistence and Environmental Stability. Long-chain PFAS demonstrate greater resistance to leaching under steady-state flow conditions but may exhibit delayed pulse releases when hydrological perturbations disrupt equilibrium conditions [CITE:ev_f465951926ec5f89]. This \"resistance to leaching\" refers specifically to retarded vertical transport rather than immobilization; long-chain compounds remain susceptible to episodic mobilization as addressed in discussions of persistent secondary sources. Short-chain compounds achieve substantial porewater concentrations due to higher initial aqueous solubility and more rapid desorption kinetics—properties examined in Surface Activity and Solubility Controls on Foam Formation and Stability—enabling faster breakthrough to underlying aquifers [CITE:ev_f465951926ec5f89].\n\nThe relationship between soil organic carbon content and PFAS retention follows modified partitioning models accounting for the amphiphilic nature of these contaminants [CITE:ev_f465951926ec5f89]. Unlike nonpolar organic compounds where sorption correlates linearly with organic carbon fraction, PFAS partitioning incorporates electrostatic interactions with mineral surfaces and specific binding to charged soil constituents [CITE:ev_f465951926ec5f89]. Long-chain perfluoroalkyl acids particularly exhibit this dual-mode sorption behavior, partitioning into organic carbon domains while simultaneously coordinating with metal oxide surfaces through their charged headgroups [CITE:ev_f465951926ec5f89]. Short-chain analogs participate less effectively in hydrophobic partitioning, rendering their transport more sensitive to pH-dependent surface charge variations and ionic strength effects [CITE:ev_f465951926ec5f89].\n\nVadose zone processes preceding aquifer contamination include multiple retention mechanisms operating at distinct temporal scales [CITE:ev_f465951926ec5f89]. Immediate sorption to mineral surfaces occurs within hours, followed by slower diffusion into soil organic matter and intraparticle micropores over days to weeks [CITE:ev_f465951926ec5f89]. Long-chain PFAS penetrate these immobile domains more extensively due to favorable free energy of transfer, effectively removing them from mobile porewater fractions on timescales relevant to episodic infiltration events [CITE:ev_f465951926ec5f89]. Short-chain compounds remain predominantly in rapidly exchanging pools, maintaining higher effective mobility despite lower affinity per sorption site [CITE:ev_f465951926ec5f89].\n\nSites with historical long-chain PFAS releases may exhibit delayed groundwater impacts relative to short-chain contamination, not due to absence of source term but due to vadose zone retention capacity [CITE:ev_f465951926ec5f89]. Short-chain replacement compounds introduced after regulatory phase-outs demonstrate more rapid breakthrough to groundwater, potentially underestimating transport velocities calculated from conservative tracer behavior [CITE:ev_f465951926ec5f89]. This temporal decoupling between surface release and groundwater detection complicates source attribution and remedial prioritization, as detection timing does not necessarily reflect release chronology.\n\nTreatment implications extend directly from these mechanistic understandings. Ex-situ soil washing and in-situ flushing technologies must account for chain-length-dependent desorption kinetics; long-chain PFAS require more aggressive chemical enhancement or extended residence times to achieve equivalent removal efficiency [CITE:ev_f465951926ec5f89]. The mobile porewater fraction—directly available for groundwater migration—contains disproportionate short-chain enrichment relative to total soil composition, suggesting that monitoring focused on leachable rather than total concentrations would better predict aquifer contamination risk [CITE:ev_f465951926ec5f89]. Remediation strategies targeting the vadose zone must incorporate chain-length-specific extraction protocols rather than assuming uniform contaminant behavior across PFAS classes [CITE:ev_f465951926ec5f89].\n\n## Sediment Contamination Hotspots: Formation and Secondary Source Potential\n\n Sediment contamination hotspots function as persistent secondary sources capable of sustaining aquifer recontamination over extended temporal scales. These hotspots form preferentially near the sediment surface in zones immediately downstream from point source discharges, creating localized zones of elevated PFAA concentration that persist beyond primary source remediation [CITE:ev_9b490b7f35013689]. This surface-proximal localization carries particular significance for aquifer vulnerability, as these shallow reservoirs maintain direct hydraulic connectivity with underlying groundwater systems through hyporheic exchange and vertical infiltration pathways—a dynamic explored in detail in the discussion of soil-porewater partitioning mechanisms in unsaturated zones.\n\nThe secondary source potential of sediment hotspots derives from their capacity to release accumulated PFAS mass following perturbations to prevailing geochemical conditions. Unlike primary sources where contamination inputs occur through direct discharge, secondary release from sediments operates through desorption-driven mechanisms triggered by changes in porewater chemistry, flow regime alterations, or competitive displacement by co-contaminants—processes that parallel the partitioning dynamics governing PFAS mobility in unsaturated soil systems. This distinction fundamentally shapes remediation prioritization, as hotspot sediments require active intervention rather than passive source control measures.\n\nLong-chain PFAS demonstrate substantially stronger sediment retention and more efficient hotspot formation near discharge points, where these compounds accumulate to higher concentrations within smaller spatial footprints. Conversely, short-chain PFAS demonstrate greater downstream transport capacity, distributing contamination across broader sediment reaches rather than concentrating in discrete hotspots. The bioaccumulation-based classification scheme previously established for PFAS categorization reinforces this functional divergence in environmental partitioning behavior.\n\nThe implications for aquifer recontamination risk are substantial. Long-chain PFAS hotspots represent high-concentration, spatially constrained sources; however, the magnitude of flux to underlying aquifers depends on site-specific hydraulic gradients and mass transfer kinetics rather than concentration alone, reflecting the complex relationship between soil-phase concentrations and groundwater loading documented in soil-porewater partitioning research. Short-chain PFAS, despite forming less pronounced hotspots, create diffuse contamination zones that sustain chronic low-level loading across extended groundwater capture areas. Neither scenario permits simple risk assessment based solely on total mass calculations; rather, the spatial configuration of contamination relative to groundwater flow paths determines secondary source significance. Research quantifying PFAS mass transfer between groundwater and surface water using field data addresses critical gaps in understanding these transport dynamics [CITE:ev_b6ec9c67e6c7f2da].\n\nTreatment implications diverge markedly between hotspot types. Long-chain enriched sediments may respond to targeted extraction technologies leveraging their strong surface affinity. Foam fractionation techniques have demonstrated capacity to reduce total PFAS concentrations in natural groundwaters from approximately 15,000 ng L−1 to below detection limits of 8 ng L−1 within 15 minutes, suggesting applicability for concentrated hotspot remediation [CITE:ev_af452886ae47c28f]. Short-chain contaminated sediments present substantially greater technical challenges given their weak retention across environmental matrices, necessitating more aggressive and costly remediation approaches. The differential treatability of hotspot sediments reinforces the economic and technical advantages of preventing long-chain PFAS discharge relative to managing distributed short-chain contamination.\n\nTemporal dynamics of hotspot reactivation warrant particular attention in aquifer protection planning. Sediment PFAS reservoirs can maintain release potential over decadal timescales, substantially exceeding the operational lifetimes of typical source control measures. This persistence creates legacy contamination scenarios where aquifer systems continue receiving PFAS loading decades after primary source cessation, complicating attainment of groundwater quality objectives. The chain-length composition of hotspot sediments directly influences this temporal dimension, with long-chain compounds exhibiting slower release kinetics that prolong secondary source activity.\n\nUnderstanding hotspot formation and secondary source potential provides essential context for interpreting groundwater concentration baselines, particularly in distinguishing true background conditions from legacy contamination signals. Elevated aquifer PFAS concentrations in areas lacking identifiable current sources frequently reflect sediment hotspot reactivation rather than undetected ongoing discharge—a consideration central to establishing defensible groundwater concentration baselines. This recognition shifts monitoring priorities toward sediment characterization and hyporheic zone assessment in groundwater protection strategies.\n\n## Air-Water Interfacial Dynamics: Differential Accumulation by Chain Length\n\n Air-water interfacial partitioning establishes a primary mechanism by which chain length governs PFAS transport through unsaturated zones. Long-chain compounds including PFOA and PFOS exhibit substantially greater accumulation at air-water interfaces than their short-chain counterparts PFBS and PFBA [CITE:ev_ea0a3d3c15926396]. This differential accumulation arises from amplified amphiphilic character with chain extension—longer fluorocarbon tails increasingly orient toward non-aqueous phases while charged head groups remain water-anchored, creating thermodynamically favorable conditions for interface residence [CITE:ev_ea0a3d3c15926396].\n\nThe environmental significance of this interfacial accumulation extends to soil distribution patterns. Research examining PFAS partitioning between bulk soil matrices and porewater demonstrates that air-water interfacial adsorption governs long-chain PFAS localization within soil samples [CITE:ev_7a39778821ebcf06]. Soil pores contain extensive interfacial area, particularly under intermediate moisture conditions, providing substantial retention capacity that operates independently of mineral surface sorption or organic matter partitioning [CITE:ev_7a39778821ebcf06]. This mechanism represents an additional partitioning pathway that short-chain compounds largely cannot exploit [CITE:ev_ea0a3d3c15926396].\n\nLong-chain PFAS do not merely experience transport retardation; they partition into distinct physical domains that fundamentally alter migration pathways. The air-water interface functions as a temporary reservoir that buffers long-chain compounds against rapid leaching while simultaneously generating concentration gradients that drive lateral diffusion and non-equilibrium transport behaviors [CITE:ev_7a39778821ebcf06]. Consequently, long-chain PFAS exhibit pronounced depth stratification in soil profiles, with accumulation near source zones and diminished penetration to groundwater, whereas short-chain homologues demonstrate more uniform vertical distribution extending deeper into the profile [CITE:ev_7a39778821ebcf06]—a pattern with direct implications for groundwater concentration baselines discussed in Section s07 (Groundwater Concentration Baselines and Background Levels).\n\nThis divergence creates a fundamental asymmetry in aquifer contamination potential. Chain length inversely correlates with interfacial accumulation but directly correlates with environmental persistence, establishing opposing controls on leaching behavior [CITE:ev_ea0a3d3c15926396]. The interfacial accumulation effect competes with dissolution-driven transport, sequestering long-chain compounds in stationary phases that resist advective mobilization [CITE:ev_7a39778821ebcf06]. Short-chain PFAS, lacking comparable interfacial affinity, remain preferentially dissolved in porewater and thus more susceptible to vertical transport through gravitational drainage and capillary-driven flow [CITE:ev_ea0a3d3c15926396].\n\nFoam fractionation selectively exploits this interfacial accumulation behavior for long-chain PFAS removal, with CTAB-enhanced systems demonstrating capacity to reduce total PFAS concentrations from approximately 15,000 ng L−1 to below 8 ng L−1 detection limits within 15 minutes [CITE:ev_af452886ae47c28f]. This mechanistic linkage—detailed in Section s05 (Surface Activity and Solubility Controls on Foam Fractionation and Solution Retention)—indicates that remediation approaches effective for long-chain PFAS removal from water simultaneously reveal where these compounds accumulate in subsurface environments [CITE:ev_ea0a3d3c15926396]. Conversely, weak interfacial accumulation of short-chain PFAS limits both their foam fractionation recoverability and their natural attenuation through vadose zone retention [CITE:ev_ea0a3d3c15926396].\n\nTransport models that incorporate only equilibrium sorption to solid phases systematically underestimate long-chain PFAS retention anrg: outputs\codex_pytest_base: Access is denied. tests\fixtures\subagent_prompts\section_writer_response.txt:1:Building on the clinical rationale for non-pharmacological glycemic strategies, this section examines how intermittent fasting (IF) modifies the metabolic pathways most relevant to type 2 diabetes mellitus (T2DM), drawing on weighted and standardized mean differences from systematic reviews that aggregate over 50 clinical studies comparing IF protocols against caloric restriction and usual-diet controls [CITE:ev_12e3ca8d07d9d3ed].
tests\fixtures\subagent_prompts\section_writer_response.txt:5:Weight reduction is the most consistently observed outcome across IF trials, and it is mechanistically central to the improvement of insulin resistance in T2DM. In adults with overweight or obesity, IF reduced fat mass by a mean difference of −0.72 kg compared with a non-intervention diet [CITE:ev_243de27dc515c169]. When IF is combined with structured physical exercise, the weight reduction relative to a control diet plus exercise reaches a weighted mean difference of −1.83 kg (P = 0.001) [CITE:ev_749f00cb5c5eea28], suggesting an additive effect when caloric timing is paired with energy expenditure. The largest effect size is observed with modified alternate-day fasting, which produced a weight reduction of −5.18 kg (95% CI: −7.04 to −3.32) compared with a usual diet, supported by high certainty of evidence [CITE:ev_e7bdb4797d3f6f0f]. The differential between these protocols is clinically meaningful: a five-kilogram loss falls within the range associated with measurable improvements in HbA1c and insulin sensitivity, whereas sub-kilogram fat-mass reductions, although statistically significant, may yield more modest glycemic benefits.
tests\fixtures\subagent_prompts\section_writer_response.txt:11:The lipid response to IF reinforces its cardiometabolic relevance for the T2DM population, where dyslipidemia is a frequent comorbidity. When IF is combined with exercise, the reduction is larger and reported in absolute units: a weighted mean difference of −5.35 mg/dL (P = 0.03) for LDL cholesterol [CITE:ev_39b4891376876856]. Triacylglycerols also fall under IF protocols, with a standardized mean difference of −0.23 (95% CI: −0.39 to −0.06; p = 0.007) [CITE:ev_e273c28dab55ecd8]. The convergence of LDL and triglyceride reductions is consistent with the broader observation that IF exerts beneficial effects on the lipid profile [CITE:ev_dde1177bb61ef9e9], a finding that strengthens its candidacy as an adjunctive intervention for individuals with T2DM whose treatment goals extend beyond glycemic control to encompass cardiovascular risk attenuation.
tests\fixtures\subagent_prompts\section_writer_response.txt:28:The table makes the protocol-dependent gradient explicit. state\codex_consultations\bootstrap_round2_response.md:791:.\outputs\audits\v25\_codex_stdout.txt:1134:   17: In a phase 1 clinical trial specifically designed to assess islet function and insulin sensitivity, subcutaneous tirzepatide demonstrated effects on pancreatic islet function versus placebo or semaglutide in adults with type 2 diabetes.[16] GIP's role is nuanced; it stimulates glucose-dependent insulin secretion and, uniquely, also stimulates glucagon secretion during hyperglycemia, which one review suggests may subsequently enhance insulin secretion through intra-islet paracrine communication.[17] This contrasts with the historical view that GIP's glucagon stimulation was uniformly detrimental in type 2 diabetes.[17] In phase 2 trials, tirzepatide demonstrated dose-dependent improvements in beta-cell function, as measured by the Homeostatic Model Assessment 2 of beta-cell function (HOMA2-B) index.[18] The combined incretin action results in robust glycemic control, with one review of the SURPASS trials reporting that a greater proportion of participants receiving tirzepatide achieved HbA1c levels below 5.7% across baseline HbA1c categories compared to GLP-1 receptor agonists.[19]

state\codex_consultations\bootstrap_round2_response.md:1294:.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_14_v27\_codex_stdout.txt:1493:outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_9\codex_stderr.log:156:Tirzepatide is approved by the FDA under the proprietary name Mounjaro as an adjunct to diet and exercise to improve glycemic control in adults with type 2 diabetes mellitus.[24] The FDA-approved dosing regimen starts at 2.5 mg subcutaneously once weekly, with increases in 2.5 mg increments after a minimum of 4 weeks on the current dose, up to a maximum of 15 mg once weekly.[24][25] The European Medicines Agency (EMA) authorizes tirzepatide (Mounjaro) for the same T2DM indication in adults, as monotherapy when metformin is inappropriate or as an add-on to other antidiabetic medicines.[26][27] The EMA also approves tirzepatide for weight management as an adjunct to a reduced-calorie diet and increased physical activity in adults with an initial BMI of ≥30 kg/m² (obesity) or ≥27 kg/m² to <30 kg/m² (overweight) in the presence of at least one weight-related comorbidity.[28][26] For the chronic weight management indication, the FDA approved tirzepatide under the name Zepbound, with an identical starting and titration schedule up to 15 mg once weekly.[29][30] A key safety warning from both agencies is a boxed warning for risk of thyroid C-cell tumors, as tirzepatide caused thyroid C-cell tumors in rats, though human relevance is unknown.[31][32] Consequently, tirzepatide is contraindicated in patients with a personal or family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma (MTC) or in patients with Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia syndrome type 2 (MEN 2).[31][32] Other warnings and precautions common to the labels include the risk of severe gastrointestinal adverse reactions, acute pancreatitis, hypoglycemia with concomitant insulin secretagogues or insulin, hypersensitivity reactions, acute kidney injury, acute gallbladder disease, and potential impact on absorption of oral medications due to delayed gastric emptying.[31][32] The EMA product information additionally notes a mean increase in heart rate and includes diabetic retinopathy complications and pulmonary aspiration during anesthesia as precautions.[26] The UK's National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) recommends tirzepatide for treating T2DM in adults only under specific conditions: when triple therapy with metformin and two other oral antidiabetic drugs is ineffective, not tolerated, or contraindicated, and the patient has a BMI of 35 kg/m² or more with associated psychological or medical problems, or a BMI under 35 kg/m² where insulin therapy has significant occupational implications or weight loss would benefit other obesity-related complications.[33][34] NICE guidance also specifies using lower BMI thresholds for people from certain ethnic backgrounds.[33] For weight management, NICE provides a prescribing guide, confirming the starting dose of 2.5 mg once weekly and titration by 2.5 mg every 4 weeks to recommended maintenance doses of 5 mg, 10 mg, or 15 mg once weekly.[35]
state\codex_consultations\bootstrap_round2_response.md:1331:.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_11\_codex_stdout_compact.txt:168:  17: Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved tirzepatide under the proprietary name Mounjaro on May 15, 2022, as an adjunct to diet and exercise to improve glycemic control in adults with type 2 diabetes mellitus.[17] The FDA-approved dosing regimen for this indication starts at 2.5 mg injected subcutaneously once weekly, with the dose increased to 5 mg after 4 weeks, and further increased in 2.5 mg increments after a minimum of 4 weeks on the current dose as needed for glycemic control, up to a maximum of 15 mg once weekly.[17][18] Subsequently, the FDA approved tirzepatide under the proprietary name Zepbound on November 8, 2023, for chronic weight management as an adjunct to a reduced-calorie diet and increased physical activity in adults with an initial body mass index (BMI) of 30 kg/m² or greater (obesity) or 27 kg/m² or greater (overweight) in the presence of at least one weight-related comorbid condition such as hypertension, dyslipidemia, type 2 diabetes mellitus, obstructive sleep apnea, or cardiovascular disease.[19][20] The FDA-approved dosing for weight management follows the same titration schedule, starting at 2.5 mg once weekly and escalating to maintenance doses of 5 mg, 10 mg, or 15 mg once weekly, with the 2.5 mg dose intended only for treatment initiation and not for chronic management.[20][21] The European Medicines Agency (EMA) granted a marketing authorization for tirzepatide (Mounjaro) on September 15, 2022, for the treatment of adults with insufficiently controlled type 2 diabetes as an adjunct to diet and exercise, either as monotherapy when metformin is inappropriate or in addition to other antidiabetic medicinal products.[22][23] The EMA's approved indication was later expanded to include weight management as an adjunct to a reduced-calorie diet and increased physical activity for weight management, including weight loss and weight maintenance, in adults with an initial BMI of ≥30 kg/m² (obesity) or ≥27 kg/m² to <30 kg/m² (overweight) with at least one weight-related comorbidity.[24][22] The FDA prescribing information for Mounjaro carries a boxed warning for the risk of thyroid C-cell tumors, noting that tirzepatide caused dose-dependent and treatment-duration-dependent thyroid C-cell tumors in rats, and it is contraindicated in patients with a personal or family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma (MTC) or in patients with Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia syndrome type 2 (MEN 2).[18] This warning is also reflected in the EMA's product information, which advises that tirzepatide is contraindicated in patients with a personal or family history of MTC or in patients with MEN 2.[22] Both agencies list warnings and precautions for acute pancreatitis, hypoglycemia with concomitant use of insulin secretagogues or insulin, hypersensitivity reactions, acute kidney injury, severe gastrointestinal adverse reactions, diabetic retinopathy complications in patients with a history thereof, acute gallbladder disease, and pulmonary aspiration during anesthesia or deep sedation.[18][22] The FDA label specifically states that Mounjaro is not recommended in patients with severe gastroparesis.[18] The National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) in England recommends tirzepatide for treating type 2 diabetes alongside diet and exercise in adults only under specific criteria: when triple therapy with metformin and two other oral antidiabetic drugs is ineffective, not tolerated, or contraindicated, and the patient has a BMI of 35 kg/m² or more with specific psychological or other medical problems associated with obesity, or a BMI of less than 35 kg/m² where insulin therapy would have significant occupational implications or weight loss would benefit other significant obesity-related complications.[25][26] NICE guidance specifies using lower BMI thresholds, usually reduced by 2.5 kg/m², for people from South Asian, Chinese, other Asian, Middle Eastern, Black African, or African-Caribbean family backgrounds.[25][26] The NICE guidance for weight management advises a starting dose of 2.5 mg once weekly, with titration by 2.5 mg every 4 weeks to recommended maintenance doses of 5 mg, 10 mg, or 15 mg once weekly, and recommends assessing treatment response after 6 months on the highest tolerated dose.[27] The EMA's assessment similarly relied on the SURPASS trial program for the diabetes indication and the SURMOUNT trials for weight management.[24][28][23] The pharmacokinetic profile supporting the once-weekly dosing is consistent across agencies, with a mean half-life of approximately 5 days, an absolute bioavailability of about 80%, and steady-state concentrations achieved after 4 weeks of administration.[21][29][22]
state\codex_consultations\bootstrap_round2_response.md:1340:.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_11\_codex_stdout_compact.txt:3236:  17: Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved tirzepatide under the proprietary name Mounjaro on May 15, 2022, as an adjunct to diet and exercise to improve glycemic control in adults with type 2 diabetes mellitus.[17] The FDA-approved dosing regimen for this indication starts at 2.5 mg injected subcutaneously once weekly, with the dose increased to 5 mg after 4 weeks, and further increased in 2.5 mg increments after a minimum of 4 weeks on the current dose as needed for glycemic control, up to a maximum of 15 mg once weekly.[17][18] Subsequently, the FDA approved tirzepatide under the proprietary name Zepbound on November 8, 2023, for chronic weight management as an adjunct to a reduced-calorie diet and increased physical activity in adults with an initial body mass index (BMI) of 30 kg/m² or greater (obesity) or 27 kg/m² or greater (overweight) in the presence of at least one weight-related comorbid condition such as hypertension, dyslipidemia, type 2 diabetes mellitus, obstructive sleep apnea, or cardiovascular disease.[19][20] The FDA-approved dosing for weight management follows the same titration schedule, starting at 2.5 mg once weekly and escalating to maintenance doses of 5 mg, 10 mg, or 15 mg once weekly, with the 2.5 mg dose intended only for treatment initiation and not for chronic management.[20][21] The European Medicines Agency (EMA) granted a marketing authorization for tirzepatide (Mounjaro) on September 15, 2022, for the treatment of adults with insufficiently controlled type 2 diabetes as an adjunct to diet and exercise, either as monotherapy when metformin is inappropriate or in addition to other antidiabetic medicinal products.[22][23] The EMA's approved indication was later expanded to include weight management as an adjunct to a reduced-calorie diet and increased physical activity for weight management, including weight loss and weight maintenance, in adults with an initial BMI of ≥30 kg/m² (obesity) or ≥27 kg/m² to <30 kg/m² (overweight) with at least one weight-related comorbidity.[24][22] The FDA prescribing information for Mounjaro carries a boxed warning for the risk of thyroid C-cell tumors, noting that tirzepatide caused dose-dependent and treatment-duration-dependent thyroid C-cell tumors in rats, and it is contraindicated in patients with a personal or family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma (MTC) or in patients with Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia syndrome type 2 (MEN 2).[18] This warning is also reflected in the EMA's product information, which advises that tirzepatide is contraindicated in patients with a personal or family history of MTC or in patients with MEN 2.[22] Both agencies list warnings and precautions for acute pancreatitis, hypoglycemia with concomitant use of insulin secretagogues or insulin, hypersensitivity reactions, acute kidney injury, severe gastrointestinal adverse reactions, diabetic retinopathy complications in patients with a history thereof, acute gallbladder disease, and pulmonary aspiration during anesthesia or deep sedation.[18][22] The FDA label specifically states that Mounjaro is not recommended in patients with severe gastroparesis.[18] The National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) in England recommends tirzepatide for treating type 2 diabetes alongside diet and exercise in adults only under specific criteria: when triple therapy with metformin and two other oral antidiabetic drugs is ineffective, not tolerated, or contraindicated, and the patient has a BMI of 35 kg/m² or more with specific psychological or other medical problems associated with obesity, or a BMI of less than 35 kg/m² where insulin therapy would have significant occupational implications or weight loss would benefit other significant obesity-related complications.[25][26] NICE guidance specifies using lower BMI thresholds, usually reduced by 2.5 kg/m², for people from South Asian, Chinese, other Asian, Middle Eastern, Black African, or African-Caribbean family backgrounds.[25][26] The NICE guidance for weight management advises a starting dose of 2.5 mg once weekly, with titration by 2.5 mg every 4 weeks to recommended maintenance doses of 5 mg, 10 mg, or 15 mg once weekly, and recommends assessing treatment response after 6 months on the highest tolerated dose.[27] The EMA's assessment similarly relied on the SURPASS trial program for the diabetes indication and the SURMOUNT trials for weight management.[24][28][23] The pharmacokinetic profile supporting the once-weekly dosing is consistent across agencies, with a mean half-life of approximately 5 days, an absolute bioavailability of about 80%, and steady-state concentrations achieved after 4 weeks of administration.[21][29][22]

pulmonary aspiration during anesthesia or deep sedation.[18][22] The FDA label specifically states that Mounjaro is not recommended in patients with severe gastroparesis.[18] The National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) in England recommends tirzepatide for treating type 2 diabetes alongside diet and exercise in adults only under specific criteria: when triple therapy with metformin and two other oral antidiabetic drugs is ineffective, not tolerated, or contraindicated, and the patient has a BMI of 35 kg/m² or more with specific psychological or other medical problems associated with obesity, or a BMI of less than 35 kg/m² where insulin therapy would have significant occupational implications or weight loss would benefit other significant obesity-related complications.[25][26] NICE guidance specifies using lower BMI thresholds, usually reduced by 2.5 kg/m², for people from South Asian, Chinese, other Asian, Middle Eastern, Black African, or African-Caribbean family backgrounds.[25][26] The NICE guidance for weight management advises a starting dose of 2.5 mg once weekly, with titration by 2.5 mg every 4 weeks to recommended maintenance doses of 5 mg, 10 mg, or 15 mg once weekly, and recommends assessing treatment response after 6 months on the highest tolerated dose.[27] The EMA's assessment similarly relied on the SURPASS trial program for the diabetes indication and the SURMOUNT trials for weight management.[24][28][23] The pharmacokinetic profile supporting the once-weekly dosing is consistent across agencies, with a mean Inclusion in an NLM database does not imply endorsement of, or agreement with, the contents by NLM or the National Institutes of Health.  \nLearn more: [PMC Disclaimer](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/about/disclaimer/) | [ PMC Copyright Notice ](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/about/copyright/)\n![Biomedical Journal logo](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/banners/logo-biomedicalj.gif)\nBiomed J\n. 2023 Nov 7;47(1):100677. doi: [10.1016/j.bj.2023.100677](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.bj.2023.100677)\n  * [Search in PMC](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/search/?term=\"Biomed%20J\"\\[jour\\])\n  * [Search in PubMed](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=\"Biomed%20J\"\\[jour\\])\n  * [View in NLM Catalog](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/nlmcatalog?term=\"Biomed%20J\"\\[Title%20Abbreviation\\])\n  * [Add to search](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/?term=\"Biomed%20J\"\\[jour\\])\n\n# Impact of fasting & ketogenic interventions on the NLRP3 inflammasome: A narrative review\n[Helena Neudorf](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=\"Neudorf%20H\"\\[Author\\])\n### Helena Neudorf\n1University of British Columbia, Okanagan Campus, Kelowna, BC, Canada\nFind articles by [Helena Neudorf](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=\"Neudorf%20H\"\\[Author\\])\n1, [Jonathan P Little](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=\"Little%20JP\"\\[Author\\])\n### Jonathan P Little\n1University of British Columbia, Okanagan Campus, Kelowna, BC, Canada\nFind articles by [Jonathan P Little](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=\"Little%20JP\"\\[Author\\])\n1,\u2217\n  * Author information\n  * Article notes\n  * Copyright and License information\n\n1University of British Columbia, Okanagan Campus, Kelowna, BC, Canada\n\u2217\nCorresponding author. University of British Columbia, Okanagan Campus, Kelowna, BC, Canada. jonathan.little@ubc.ca\nReceived 2023 Jun 29; Revised 2023 Oct 27; Accepted 2023 Oct 30; Issue date 2024 Feb.\n\u00a9 2023 The Authors\nThis is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/).\n[PMC Copyright notice](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/about/copyright/)\nPMCID: PMC10821592 PMID: [37940045](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/37940045/)\n##  Abstract\nOveractivation of the NLRP3 inflammasome is implicated in chronic low-grade inflammation associated with various disease states, including obesity, type 2 diabetes, atherosclerosis, Alzheimer's disease, and Parkinson's disease. Emerging evidence, mostly from cell and animal models of disease, supports a role for ketosis in general, and the main circulating ketone body beta-hydroxybutyrate (BHB) in particular, in reducing NLRP3 inflammasome activation to improve chronic inflammation. As a result, interventions that can induce ketosis (e.g., fasting, intermittent fasting, time-restricted feeding/eating, very low-carbohydrate high-fat ketogenic diets) and/or increase circulating BHB (e.g., exogenous ketone supplementation) have garnered increasing interest for their therapeutic potential. The purpose of the present review is to summarize our current understanding of the literature on how ketogenic interventions impact the NLRP3 inflammasome across human, rodent and cell models. Overall, there is convincing evidence that ketogenic interventions, likely acting through multiple interacting mechanisms in a cell-, disease- and context-specific manner, can reduce NLRP3 inflammasome activation. The evidence supports a direct effect of BHB, although it is important to consider the myriad of other metabolic responses to fasting or ketogenic diet interventions (e.g., elevated lipolysis, low insulin, stable glucose, negative energy balance) that may also impact innate immune responses. Future research is needed to translate promising findings from discovery science to clinical application.\n**Keywords:** Ketogenic diets, NLRP3 inflammasome, Fasting, Beta-hydroxybutyrate, Inflammation\n##  Highlights\n  * \u2022\nFasting and ketogenic interventions suppress NLRP3 activation, likely via BHB\n  * \u2022\nBHB inhibits NLRP3 activation through multiple mechanisms\n  * \u2022\nBHB inhibits K+ efflux, ROS, and ER stress-induced NLRP3 inflammasome activation\n  * \u2022\nBHB likely acts differentially across species, diseases and physiological contexts\n\n##  1. Introduction\n### 1.1. Relevance of the NLRP3 inflammasome in health & disease\nPersistent low-grade inflammation, largely driven by overactivation of cells of the innate immune system, is now recognized to play a significant role in many chronic and aging-associated diseases, including obesity, type 2 diabetes (T2D), cardiovascular disease (CVD), and Alzheimer's disease. Understanding the cellular and molecular processes underpinning such chronic inflammatory disease states is therefore important as it may guide potential (anti-inflammatory) treatments. Indeed, such understanding has led to anti-inflammatory therapy such as interleukin (IL)-1 receptor blockade or IL-1\u03b2 antagonists, which block pro-inflammatory IL-1\u03b2 signaling, with randomized controlled trials showing slower progression of CVD and T2D using such agents [[1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib1),[2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib2)]. The NOD-like receptor (NLR) family pyrin domain containing 3 (NLRP3) inflammasome is one molecular pathway involved in innate immune activation that has been repeatedly implicated in chronic inflammation associated with various disease states [[3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib3)]. The detailed molecular mechanisms of NLRP3 signaling and activation have been extensively described in several recent reviews [[[4]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib4), [[5]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib5), [[6]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib6), [[7]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib7), [[8]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib8)] and a summary is presented in [[Fig. 1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#fig1)].\n#### Fig. 1.\n[![Fig.\u00a01](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/blobs/6610/10821592/dfad6d2864d0/gr1.jpg)](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/core/lw/2.0/html/tileshop_pmc/tileshop_pmc_inline.html?title=Click%20on%20image%20to%20zoom&p=PMC3&id=10821592_gr1.jpg)\n[Open in a new tab](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/figure/fig1/)\nMechanistic overview of NLRP3 inflammasome signalling. Abbreviations:ASC: associated speck-like protein; CARD: caspase activation and recruitment domain; FADD:Fas-associated death domain protein; IKK:IkB kinase; IL: interleukin; LPS: lipopolysaccharide; MyD88: myeloid differentiation primary response 88; NACHT: NAIP (neuronal apoptosis inhibitor protein), C2TA (class 2 transcription activator, of the MHC), HET-E (heterokaryon incompatibility) and TP1 (telomerase-associated protein 1); Nek7: never in mitosis gene A (NIMA)-related kinase; NF-kB: nuclear factor k-light-chain-enhancer of activated B cells; NLRP3:NOD-like receptor pyrin domain containing 3; PYD: pyrin domain; LRR: C-terminal receptor domain of leucine-rich repeats; RIPK1: receptor-interacting serine/threonine-protein kinase 1; TLR4: toll-like receptor 4; TRIF: toll-interleukin-1 receptor (TIR) domain containing adaptor inducing interferon beta.\nBriefly, a danger- or pathogen-associated molecular pattern (DAMP or PAMP) binds its ligand (e.g., toll-like receptor (TLR)) which induces activation of nuclear factor kappa-light-chain-enhancer of activated B cells (NF-\u03baB) and consequent transcription of NLRP3 and IL1B mRNA. In canonical NLRP3 signalling, a second activating signal is required following the initial priming signal. The second signal induces NLRP3 oligomerization and recruitment of NLRP3, associated speck-like protein (ASC) and pro-caspase-1. Pro-caspase-1 is cleaved autoproteolytically, and the resulting caspase-1 cleaves pro-IL-1\u03b2 and pro-IL-18 into their active forms, which are then secreted from the cell [[3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib3)]. It is important to acknowledge that our understanding of the mechanisms by which the NLRP3 inflammasome is activated is largely based on studies in rodents, and evidence suggests that alternative mechanisms of activation exist in human immune cells in addition to the canonical two-step model [[9](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib9)]. Importantly, in human monocytes, a single TLR4-mediated stimulus is sufficient to activate the NLRP3 inflammasome, which can occur independently of caspase-4 and K+ efflux. This single-step model of activation, coined \u201calternative activation\u201d, induces a more moderated inflammatory response in which pyroptosis does not occur and less IL-1\u03b2 is secreted [[9](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib9)]. A final \u201cnon-canonical\u201d mode of activation has also been identified in which rodent caspase-11 is also activated by a single TLR4-mediated stimulus and activates NLRP3 directly via K+ efflux [[[9]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib9), [[10]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib10), [[11]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib11), [[12]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib12)]. Despite these multiple modes of NLRP3 inflammasome activation, the ultimate release of pro-inflammatory cytokines remains a tightly regulated process.\nNumerous studies, in both rodent and human models, have reported heightened NLRP3 activation in conditions where chronic low-grade inflammation is implicated in disease pathophysiology [[Fig. 2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#fig2)]. This includes NLRP3 in adipose tissue-infiltrating macrophages, which contribute to the development of insulin resistance and low-grade inflammation in obesity [[3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib3)] and in pancreas-infiltrating macrophages, which respond to a myriad of metabolites (e.g., ceramides, saturated fatty acids, hyperglycemia), reactive oxygen species (ROS), and aggregated islet amyloid polypeptide (IAPP) contributing to beta cell death/dysfunction in T2D [[3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib3),[13](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib13)]. NLRP3 is also implicated in atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease. Macrophage NLRP3 in atherosclerotic plaques are activated by oxidized LDL, cholesterol crystals, and calcium phosphate crystals [[3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib3),[14](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib14)]. Additionally, NLRP3 in microglia contribute to the pathogenesis of Alzheimer's disease in response to amyloid-\u03b2 and aggregated Tau [[3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib3),[15](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib15)], and microglia and astrocyte NLRP3 respond to \u03b1-synuclein via TLR2 in Parkinson's disease (PD), which is likely compounded by the loss of dopaminergic neurons which negatively regulate NLRP3 activation [[3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib3)]. In many instances, the effects observed in cells and tissues obtained from intact animals and humans can be recapitulated in cell culture models using disease-specific stimuli (e.g., IAPP for T2D, amyloid-\u03b2 for AD) suggesting that overactivation of the NLRP3 inflammasome can be specific to disease pathology. Therefore, reducing activation of the NLRP3 inflammasome is hypothesized to help lower inflammation in many chronic disease states [[3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib3)].\n#### Fig. 2.\n[![Fig.\u00a02](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/blobs/6610/10821592/76e4bea6c456/gr2.jpg)](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/core/lw/2.0/html/tileshop_pmc/tileshop_pmc_inline.html?title=Click%20on%20image%20to%20zoom&p=PMC3&id=10821592_gr2.jpg)\n[Open in a new tab](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/figure/fig2/)\nThe NLRP3 inflammasome as a central mediator of disease. AT, adipose tissue; NLRP3, NOD-like receptor pyrin domain containing 3; oxLDL, oxidized low-density lipoprotein; IAPP, islet amyloid polypeptide. Abbreviations:AT: adipose tissue; IAPP: islet amyloid polypeptide; NLRP3: NOD-like receptor pyrin domain containing 3; oxLDL: oxidized low-density lipoprotein.\nThe purpose of the present review is to summarize our current understanding of the literature on ketogenic interventions and NLRP3 across human, rodent and cell models. This review also aims to bring nuance to the broader physiological effects of ketogenic interventions and their potential effects on signalling through the NLRP3 inflammasome.\n##  2. Methods\nHere we present a narrative review with defined search criteria. All searches were conducted in the MEDLINE database. To adequately encompass the variety of names used to describe ketogenic interventions, studies were identified if they contained any of the following MeSH terms: intermittent fasting; fasting; caloric restriction; diet, carbohydrate-restricted; diet, high-protein low carbohydrate; diet, ketogenic; ketones; ketone bodies; 3-hydroxybutyric acid; or the key words \u03b2-hydroxybutyrate, high-fat low-carbohydrate diet. Studies directly quantifying NLRP3 inflammasome activation were identified if they contained any of the following MeSH terms: NLR Family, Pyrin Domain-Containing 3 Protein; Inflammasomes; or the key word NLRP3 inflammasome. Combined, these search criteria identified 102 papers. Studies were included if they were original research articles and were conducted in human or rodent cell lines, _ex vivo_ or _in vivo_ rodent models, and _ex vivo_ or _in vivo_ human models, and directly quantified NLRP3 inflammasome activation in response to ketogenic interventions in the context of non-infectious disease. Additionally, reference lists of the included papers were checked for studies that may have been missed with these search criteria. Following exclusions, 30 studies were identified as relevant by these criteria [[Table 1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#tbl1)].\n### Table 1.\nSummary of included studies Inclusion in an NLM database does not imply endorsement of, or agreement with, the contents by NLM or the National Institutes of Health.  \nLearn more: [PMC Disclaimer](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/about/disclaimer/) | [ PMC Copyright Notice ](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/about/copyright/)\n![Biomedical Journal logo](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/banners/logo-biomedicalj.gif)\nBiomed J\n. 2023 Nov 7;47(1):100677. doi: [10.1016/j.bj.2023.100677](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.bj.2023.100677)\n  * [Search in PMC](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/search/?term=\"Biomed%20J\"\\[jour\\])\n  * [Search in PubMed](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=\"Biomed%20J\"\\[jour\\])\n  * [View in NLM Catalog](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/nlmcatalog?term=\"Biomed%20J\"\\[Title%20Abbreviation\\])\n  * [Add to search](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/?term=\"Biomed%20J\"\\[jour\\])\n\n# Impact of fasting & ketogenic interventions on the NLRP3 inflammasome: A narrative review\n[Helena Neudorf](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=\"Neudorf%20H\"\\[Author\\])\n### Helena Neudorf\n1University of British Columbia, Okanagan Campus, Kelowna, BC, Canada\nFind articles by [Helena Neudorf](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=\"Neudorf%20H\"\\[Author\\])\n1, [Jonathan P Little](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=\"Little%20JP\"\\[Author\\])\n### Jonathan P Little\n1University of British Columbia, Okanagan Campus, Kelowna, BC, Canada\nFind articles by [Jonathan P Little](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=\"Little%20JP\"\\[Author\\])\n1,\u2217\n  * Author information\n  * Article notes\n  * Copyright and License information\n\n1University of British Columbia, Okanagan Campus, Kelowna, BC, Canada\n\u2217\nCorresponding author. University of British Columbia, Okanagan Campus, Kelowna, BC, Canada. jonathan.little@ubc.ca\nReceived 2023 Jun 29; Revised 2023 Oct 27; Accepted 2023 Oct 30; Issue date 2024 Feb.\n\u00a9 2023 The Authors\nThis is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/).\n[PMC Copyright notice](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/about/copyright/)\nPMCID: PMC10821592 PMID: [37940045](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/37940045/)\n##  Abstract\nOveractivation of the NLRP3 inflammasome is implicated in chronic low-grade inflammation associated with various disease states, including obesity, type 2 diabetes, atherosclerosis, Alzheimer's disease, and Parkinson's disease. Emerging evidence, mostly from cell and animal models of disease, supports a role for ketosis in general, and the main circulating ketone body beta-hydroxybutyrate (BHB) in particular, in reducing NLRP3 inflammasome activation to improve chronic inflammation. As a result, interventions that can induce ketosis (e.g., fasting, intermittent fasting, time-restricted feeding/eating, very low-carbohydrate high-fat ketogenic diets) and/or increase circulating BHB (e.g., exogenous ketone supplementation) have garnered increasing interest for their therapeutic potential. The purpose of the present review is to summarize our current understanding of the literature on how ketogenic interventions impact the NLRP3 inflammasome across human, rodent and cell models. Overall, there is convincing evidence that ketogenic interventions, likely acting through multiple interacting mechanisms in a cell-, disease- and context-specific manner, can reduce NLRP3 inflammasome activation. The evidence supports a direct effect of BHB, although it is important to consider the myriad of other metabolic responses to fasting or ketogenic diet interventions (e.g., elevated lipolysis, low insulin, stable glucose, negative energy balance) that may also impact innate immune responses. Future research is needed to translate promising findings from discovery science to clinical application.\n**Keywords:** Ketogenic diets, NLRP3 inflammasome, Fasting, Beta-hydroxybutyrate, Inflammation\n##  Highlights\n  * \u2022\nFasting and ketogenic interventions suppress NLRP3 activation, likely via BHB\n  * \u2022\nBHB inhibits NLRP3 activation through multiple mechanisms\n  * \u2022\nBHB inhibits K+ efflux, ROS, and ER stress-induced NLRP3 inflammasome activation\n  * \u2022\nBHB likely acts differentially across species, diseases and physiological contexts\n\n##  1. Introduction\n### 1.1. Relevance of the NLRP3 inflammasome in health & disease\nPersistent low-grade inflammation, largely driven by overactivation of cells of the innate immune system, is now recognized to play a significant role in many chronic and aging-associated diseases, including obesity, type 2 diabetes (T2D), cardiovascular disease (CVD), and Alzheimer's disease. Understanding the cellular and molecular processes underpinning such chronic inflammatory disease states is therefore important as it may guide potential (anti-inflammatory) treatments. Indeed, such understanding has led to anti-inflammatory therapy such as interleukin (IL)-1 receptor blockade or IL-1\u03b2 antagonists, which block pro-inflammatory IL-1\u03b2 signaling, with randomized controlled trials showing slower progression of CVD and T2D using such agents [[1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib1),[2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib2)]. The NOD-like receptor (NLR) family pyrin domain containing 3 (NLRP3) inflammasome is one molecular pathway involved in innate immune activation that has been repeatedly implicated in chronic inflammation associated with various disease states [[3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib3)]. The detailed molecular mechanisms of NLRP3 signaling and activation have been extensively described in several recent reviews [[[4]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib4), [[5]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib5), [[6]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib6), [[7]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib7), [[8]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib8)] and a summary is presented in [[Fig. 1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#fig1)].\n#### Fig. 1.\n[![Fig.\u00a01](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/blobs/6610/10821592/dfad6d2864d0/gr1.jpg)](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/core/lw/2.0/html/tileshop_pmc/tileshop_pmc_inline.html?title=Click%20on%20image%20to%20zoom&p=PMC3&id=10821592_gr1.jpg)\n[Open in a new tab](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/figure/fig1/)\nMechanistic overview of NLRP3 inflammasome signalling. Abbreviations:ASC: associated speck-like protein; CARD: caspase activation and recruitment domain; FADD:Fas-associated death domain protein; IKK:IkB kinase; IL: interleukin; LPS: lipopolysaccharide; MyD88: myeloid differentiation primary response 88; NACHT: NAIP (neuronal apoptosis inhibitor protein), C2TA (class 2 transcription activator, of the MHC), HET-E (heterokaryon incompatibility) and TP1 (telomerase-associated protein 1); Nek7: never in mitosis gene A (NIMA)-related kinase; NF-kB: nuclear factor k-light-chain-enhancer of activated B cells; NLRP3:NOD-like receptor pyrin domain containing 3; PYD: pyrin domain; LRR: C-terminal receptor domain of leucine-rich repeats; RIPK1: receptor-interacting serine/threonine-protein kinase 1; TLR4: toll-like receptor 4; TRIF: toll-interleukin-1 receptor (TIR) domain containing adaptor inducing interferon beta.\nBriefly, a danger- or pathogen-associated molecular pattern (DAMP or PAMP) binds its ligand (e.g., toll-like receptor (TLR)) which induces activation of nuclear factor kappa-light-chain-enhancer of activated B cells (NF-\u03baB) and consequent transcription of NLRP3 and IL1B mRNA. In canonical NLRP3 signalling, a second activating signal is required following the initial priming signal. The second signal induces NLRP3 oligomerization and recruitment of NLRP3, associated speck-like protein (ASC) and pro-caspase-1. Pro-caspase-1 is cleaved autoproteolytically, and the resulting caspase-1 cleaves pro-IL-1\u03b2 and pro-IL-18 into their active forms, which are then secreted from the cell [[3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib3)]. It is important to acknowledge that our understanding of the mechanisms by which the NLRP3 inflammasome is activated is largely based on studies in rodents, and evidence suggests that alternative mechanisms of activation exist in human immune cells in addition to the canonical two-step model [[9](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib9)]. Importantly, in human monocytes, a single TLR4-mediated stimulus is sufficient to activate the NLRP3 inflammasome, which can occur independently of caspase-4 and K+ efflux. This single-step model of activation, coined \u201calternative activation\u201d, induces a more moderated inflammatory response in which pyroptosis does not occur and less IL-1\u03b2 is secreted [[9](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib9)]. A final \u201cnon-canonical\u201d mode of activation has also been identified in which rodent caspase-11 is also activated by a single TLR4-mediated stimulus and activates NLRP3 directly via K+ efflux [[[9]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib9), [[10]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib10), [[11]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib11), [[12]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib12)]. Despite these multiple modes of NLRP3 inflammasome activation, the ultimate release of pro-inflammatory cytokines remains a tightly regulated process.\nNumerous studies, in both rodent and human models, have reported heightened NLRP3 activation in conditions where chronic low-grade inflammation is implicated in disease pathophysiology [[Fig. 2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#fig2)]. This includes NLRP3 in adipose tissue-infiltrating macrophages, which contribute to the development of insulin resistance and low-grade inflammation in obesity [[3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib3)] and in pancreas-infiltrating macrophages, which respond to a myriad of metabolites (e.g., ceramides, saturated fatty acids, hyperglycemia), reactive oxygen species (ROS), and aggregated islet amyloid polypeptide (IAPP) contributing to beta cell death/dysfunction in T2D [[3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib3),[13](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib13)]. NLRP3 is also implicated in atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease. Macrophage NLRP3 in atherosclerotic plaques are activated by oxidized LDL, cholesterol crystals, and calcium phosphate crystals [[3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib3),[14](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib14)]. Additionally, NLRP3 in microglia contribute to the pathogenesis of Alzheimer's disease in response to amyloid-\u03b2 and aggregated Tau [[3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib3),[15](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib15)], and microglia and astrocyte NLRP3 respond to \u03b1-synuclein via TLR2 in Parkinson's disease (PD), which is likely compounded by the loss of dopaminergic neurons which negatively regulate NLRP3 activation [[3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib3)]. In many instances, the effects observed in cells and tissues obtained from intact animals and humans can be recapitulated in cell culture models using disease-specific stimuli (e.g., IAPP for T2D, amyloid-\u03b2 for AD) suggesting that overactivation of the NLRP3 inflammasome can be specific to disease pathology. Therefore, reducing activation of the NLRP3 inflammasome is hypothesized to help lower inflammation in many chronic disease states [[3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib3)].\n#### Fig. 2.\n[![Fig.\u00a02](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/blobs/6610/10821592/76e4bea6c456/gr2.jpg)](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/core/lw/2.0/html/tileshop_pmc/tileshop_pmc_inline.html?title=Click%20on%20image%20to%20zoom&p=PMC3&id=10821592_gr2.jpg)\n[Open in a new tab](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/figure/fig2/)\nThe NLRP3 inflammasome as a central mediator of disease. AT, adipose tissue; NLRP3, NOD-like receptor pyrin domain containing 3; oxLDL, oxidized low-density lipoprotein; IAPP, islet amyloid polypeptide. Abbreviations:AT: adipose tissue; IAPP: islet amyloid polypeptide; NLRP3: NOD-like receptor pyrin domain containing 3; oxLDL: oxidized low-density lipoprotein.\nThe purpose of the present review is to summarize our current understanding of the literature on ketogenic interventions and NLRP3 across human, rodent and cell models. This review also aims to bring nuance to the broader physiological effects of ketogenic interventions and their potential effects on signalling through the NLRP3 inflammasome.\n##  2. Methods\nHere we present a narrative review with defined search criteria. All searches were conducted in the MEDLINE database. To adequately encompass the variety of names used to describe ketogenic interventions, studies were identified if they contained any of the following MeSH terms: intermittent fasting; fasting; caloric restriction; diet, carbohydrate-restricted; diet, high-protein low carbohydrate; diet, ketogenic; ketones; ketone bodies; 3-hydroxybutyric acid; or the key words \u03b2-hydroxybutyrate, high-fat low-carbohydrate diet. Studies directly quantifying NLRP3 inflammasome activation were identified if they contained any of the following MeSH terms: NLR Family, Pyrin Domain-Containing 3 Protein; Inflammasomes; or the key word NLRP3 inflammasome. Combined, these search criteria identified 102 papers. Studies were included if they were original research articles and were conducted in human or rodent cell lines, _ex vivo_ or _in vivo_ rodent models, and _ex vivo_ or _in vivo_ human models, and directly quantified NLRP3 inflammasome activation in response to ketogenic interventions in the context of non-infectious disease. Additionally, reference lists of the included papers were checked for studies that may have been missed with these search criteria. Following exclusions, 30 studies were identified as relevant by these criteria [[Table 1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#tbl1)].\n### Table 1.\nSummary of included studies state\codex_consultations\bootstrap_round2_response.md:1391:.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_9\codex_stderr.log:156:Tirzepatide is approved by the FDA under the proprietary name Mounjaro as an adjunct to diet and exercise to improve glycemic control in adults with type 2 diabetes mellitus.[24] The FDA-approved dosing regimen starts at 2.5 mg subcutaneously once weekly, with increases in 2.5 mg increments after a minimum of 4 weeks on the current dose, up to a maximum of 15 mg once weekly.[24][25] The European Medicines Agency (EMA) authorizes tirzepatide (Mounjaro) for the same T2DM indication in adults, as monotherapy when metformin is inappropriate or as an add-on to other antidiabetic medicines.[26][27] The EMA also approves tirzepatide for weight management as an adjunct to a reduced-calorie diet and increased physical activity in adults with an initial BMI of ≥30 kg/m² (obesity) or ≥27 kg/m² to <30 kg/m² (overweight) in the presence of at least one weight-related comorbidity.[28][26] For the chronic weight management indication, the FDA approved tirzepatide under the name Zepbound, with an identical starting and titration schedule up to 15 mg once weekly.[29][30] A key safety warning from both agencies is a boxed warning for risk of thyroid C-cell tumors, as tirzepatide caused thyroid C-cell tumors in rats, though human relevance is unknown.[31][32] Consequently, tirzepatide is contraindicated in patients with a personal or family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma (MTC) or in patients with Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia syndrome type 2 (MEN 2).[31][32] Other warnings and precautions common to the labels include the risk of severe gastrointestinal adverse reactions, acute pancreatitis, hypoglycemia with concomitant insulin secretagogues or insulin, hypersensitivity reactions, acute kidney injury, acute gallbladder disease, and potential impact on absorption of oral medications due to delayed gastric emptying.[31][32] The EMA product information additionally notes a mean increase in heart rate and includes diabetic retinopathy complications and pulmonary aspiration during anesthesia as precautions.[26] The UK's National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) recommends tirzepatide for treating T2DM in adults only under specific conditions: when triple therapy with metformin and two other oral antidiabetic drugs is ineffective, not tolerated, or contraindicated, and the patient has a BMI of 35 kg/m² or more with associated psychological or medical problems, or a BMI under 35 kg/m² where insulin therapy has significant occupational implications or weight loss would benefit other obesity-related complications.[33][34] NICE guidance also specifies using lower BMI thresholds for people from certain ethnic backgrounds.[33] For weight management, NICE provides a prescribing guide, confirming the starting dose of 2.5 mg once weekly and titration by 2.5 mg every 4 weeks to recommended maintenance doses of 5 mg, 10 mg, or 15 mg once weekly.[35]

state\codex_consultations\bootstrap_round2_response.md:1403:.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_6\codex_stderr.log:536:Gastrointestinal adverse events are the most common tolerability concern with tirzepatide and are typically mild to moderate in severity.[6] A systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized clinical trials reported that gastrointestinal adverse events were the most frequently reported category in tirzepatide-related individual case safety reports in a real-world pharmacovigilance database, with pancreatitis and vomiting being the most common specific events.[7] For vomiting, incidence with tirzepatide 15 mg ranged from 6% to 18.1%, and for diarrhea, it ranged from 12% to 31%.[1] One systematic review found that compared to 5 mg, tirzepatide 10 mg and 15 mg were associated with more frequent nausea, and 15 mg led to more diarrhea than 5 mg, suggesting some dose-dependence for specific gastrointestinal events.[8] The risk of hypoglycemia with tirzepatide is generally low when not used with insulin or insulin secretagogues.[9] Regarding thyroid safety, real-world pharmacovigilance data has detected a disproportionate reporting signal for medullary thyroid cancer with tirzepatide (ROR 13.67, 95% CI 4.35–42.96).[10] This aligns with a boxed warning for thyroid C-cell tumors seen in rodents, and it is unknown whether tirzepatide causes such tumors in humans, including medullary thyroid carcinoma.[11] However, the same real-world analysis noted tirzepatide exhibited a similar risk of medullary thyroid cancer compared to GLP-1 receptor agonists.[10] Furthermore, a review noted that pivotal trials in the SURPASS program did not report clinically meaningful increases in autoimmune thyroid disease or sustained thyroid dysfunction.[12] Discontinuation due to adverse events appears dose-related; one systematic review found the risk of discontinuation by adverse event was significantly higher with tirzepatide 10 mg (pooled RR=1.75, 95%CI[1.16-2.63]) and 15 mg (pooled RR=2.03, 95%CI [1.37-3.01]) compared to GLP-1 receptor agonists.[8]
